content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 20, 2006


Diana Needham
Vice-Chair
Cerritos Planning Commission
12927 Lucas Lane
Cerritos, California 90703

RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-06-152

Dear Ms. Needham:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because your request does not specify all the facts necessary for a complete analysis of the economic interests described, we can only provide you with informal assistance.² Please note that the Fair Political Practices Commission does not act as a finder of fact when providing advice; this advice is based solely on the facts that you provide. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

You are a planning commissioner considering a city-wide zoning amendment that would restrict how visible recreational vehicles may be from the street. Can you make, participate in making, or attempt to use your official position to influence the described decision?

CONCLUSION

Based upon the facts you have provided, it appears that you may have a disqualifying conflict of interest, depending upon, e.g., the nature of the real estate brokerage you work for and the effect such an amendment would have on the value of your home and the residents of your city, generally.

___________________________
¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; reg. 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)

File No. 1-06-152
Page No. 2


FACTS

You are a planning commissioner for the City of Cerritos (the "City") and Vice-
Chair of the Cerritos Planning Commission (the "Planning Commission").

Mr. Davis, a Cerritos resident, who lives in a different area of the City than you,
has requested that the city council consider adopting a city-wide zoning amendment that
would restrict the parking of recreational vehicles on private property so as to make them
less visible (or not visible at all) when viewed from the street. Mr. Davis is upset about a
large recreational vehicle parked next to his house. The city council has directed the
matter to the Planning Commission to explore crafting the zoning amendment proposed
by Mr. Davis.

You own a home, with an interest of at least $2,000. Your home is across the
street from a house where a recreational vehicle is stored and faces your home.

You will earn at least $500 from the brokerage you do work for as a real estate
agent, doing business in and around the City, in the 12 months prior to the governmental
decision at issue.³ You indicated on the telephone that you do not have an equity interest
in the brokerage for which you work. We do not know the identity of any person from
which you will have received at least $500 through your representation of them in a real
estate transaction in the 12 months prior to the decision at issue. We do not have
information regarding what, if any, persons in connection with your work as a real estate
agent, have received a finder's fee (or other referral fee) for referring a party to the
transaction to the broker, or who will have made a referral pursuant to a contract with the
broker.

There are approximately 200 recreational vehicles stored on residential lots within
Cerritos. There are approximately 15,000 homes in Cerritos.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 8100l(b).)
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence governmental decisions in which
the official has a financial interest, unless an exception applies.

³ In a telephone conversation you indicated that you own a small urban planning company, but,
because you do not make (and have not made) any money from that business while you are a planning
commissioner, that you will not have earned $500 or more from this dormant business in the 12 months
preceding the decision is made.

File No. I-06-152
Page No. 3


The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether
an official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Reg. 18700(b).) The general rule,
however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes a
governmental decision which has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on
one or more of his or her financial interests. (Section 87103.)

Steps 1 & 2: As A Planning Commissioner, Are You A Public Official
Making, Participating In Making, Or Influencing A Governmental Decision?

As a planning commissioner, you are a member or officer of a local government
agency and are a public official under the Act. (Section 82048; see reg. 18701(a)
[defining "public official"].)⁴ As a planning commissioner you will be called upon to
make, participate in making, and/or influence governmental decisions regarding the
zoning amendment you have described. (See regs. 18702 – 18702.4.) Therefore, you
will be making, participating in making, or otherwise using or attempting to use your
official position to influence a governmental decision. (See regs. 18702.1 – 18702.3.)⁵

Step 3: Do You Have A Potentially Disqualifying Economic Interest?

A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of
section 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on any of the
following types of interests:




⁴ If a public official's office is listed in section 87200 ("87200 filers" include city council
members and members of planning commissions) and he or she has a conflict of interest in a decision
noticed at a public meeting, then he or she must: (1) immediately prior to the discussion of the item,
verbally identify each type of economic interest involved in the decision as well as details of the economic
interest, as discussed in regulation 18702.5(b)(1)(B), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or
herself and leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed
sessions, consent calendars, absences and speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests,
special rules found in regulation 18702.5, subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (Section 87105.) If you want to
speak as a member of the public, you may speak only on behalf of your personal interests. (Reg.
18702.4(b).) You may not speak in a manner which, through words or actions, would lead a listener
reasonably to believe that you are speaking on behalf of another person or entity, e.g., the Planning
Commission. (Ibid.; see Hensley Advice Letter, No. A-04-168.) Since you are a member of the Planning
Commission, these requirements are applicable to you if you determine that you have a conflict-of-interest
in the zoning amendment decision.

⁵ Actions by a public official that could constitute "attempting to use his or her official position to
influence" a governmental decision before that official's agency include actions as innocuous as merely
contacting or appearing before "any member, officer, employee or consultant of the agency" if the purpose
of the contact is to influence the decision. (See reg. 18702.3.)

File No. I-06-152
Page No. 4

• An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect investment6 of $2,000 or more (section 87103(a); reg. 18703.1(a)), or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (section 87103(d); reg. 18703.1(b));

• An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (section 87103(b); reg. 18703.2; see section 82033 [defining "Interest in Real Property"]);

• An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section 87103(c); reg. 18703.3);

• An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to $360 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section 87103(e); reg. 18703.4);

• An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her immediate family -- this is the 'personal financial effects' rule. (Section 87103; reg. 18703.5.)

Based on the facts you have provided, we can identify three economic interests to analyze.

A. Real Property.

Your Home. Because you own your home, and your home is worth at least $2,000, your home constitutes an economic interest under the Act. (Sections 82033 and 87103(b); reg. 18703.2.)

B. Business Entities And Other Persons Who Are Source Of Income.

Your Income Received As A Real Estate Agent. A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of section 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on any source of income, including promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c); reg. 18703.3.) Because your job as a real estate agent, with clients in the City, will provide you with income (including promised income) of $500 or more within the 12 months prior to the governmental decision at issue, your job constitutes an economic interest under the Act. (Section 87103(c); reg. 18703.3.)

6 For purposes of section 87103, "indirect investment or interest means any investment or interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of a public official, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a business entity or trust in which the official, the official's agents, spouse, and dependent children own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest or greater." (Section 87103, last paragraph.)

File No. I-06-152
Page No. 5


Regulation 18703.3(c) contains disclosure and disqualification requirements for
any public official who receives commission income for services rendered as a real estate
agent. "Commission income" means gross payments received by a public official as a
result of services rendered as a broker, agent, or other salesperson for a specific sale or
similar transaction. (Reg. 18703.3(c)(1).) Commission income is received when it is
paid or credited. (Ibid.)

Note that, under this regulation, a number of parties in a real estate transaction are
considered sources of income for a real estate agent. (See reg. 18703.3(c)(2)(C).) That
regulation provides that the following persons and entities are all sources of income, in a
specific sale or similar transaction, under the Act:

"(i) The broker and brokerage business entity under whose auspices the
agent works;

"(ii) The person the agent represents in the transaction; and

"(iii) Any person who receives a finder's or other referral fee for referring
a party to the transaction to the broker, or who makes a referral pursuant to a
contract with the broker." (Ibid.)

You have an economic interest in the brokerage that you work under, as a source
of income, assuming you have received income from the brokerage which aggregates to
$500 or more within 12 months prior to the time the relevant decision will be made.
Other persons referenced in regulation 18703.3(c)(2)(C) may also be sources of income
to you, of course, if you receive or are promised $500 or more by any of them for
services described in this regulation.

As previously stated, we do not have information regarding what individual
persons you will have received at least $500 from through your representation of them in
real estate transactions in the 12 months prior to the decision. In addition, we do not have
information regarding what, if any, persons in connection with your work as a real estate
agent, will have received a finder's fee (or other referral fee) for referring a party to the
transaction to the broker, or who will have made a referral pursuant to a contract with the
broker that meets or exceeds the $500 / 12-month limit. Therefore, with regard to your
real estate agent commission income, we will continue to examine only the real estate
brokerage you work for as an identifiable economic interest under the Act.

_______________________________

⁷ We assume that Mr. Davis is not a source of income to you.

File No. 1-06-152
Page No. 6

C. Personal Finances.

You have an economic interest in your personal finances and those of your
immediate family. (Section 87103; reg. 18703.5; see section 82029 [defining "immediate
family" as one's spouse and dependent children].) Because you provide us with no facts
regarding the personal finances of your immediate family, we simply identify your
personal finances as a category of economic interest relevant only to you.

Step 4: Are The Economic Interests Directly Or Indirectly Involved In The
Governmental Decision?

Once an official identifies an economic interest, he or she must determine whether
the economic interest is directly or indirectly involved in the specific decision in
question. (Regs. 18700(b)(4), 18704(a).) Having established the degree of involvement,
the official must then identify the materiality standard appropriate to the circumstances.
(Reg. 18700(b)(5).) There are separate tests applied to real property, sources of income,
and personal finances, to determine whether an economic interest is directly or indirectly
involved in a specific governmental decision. We first address the test applicable to real
property.

A. Whether Real Property Is Directly Or Indirectly Involved.

Regulation 18704.2(a) states six general categories of governmental decisions, or
types of real property, in which a public official has an economic interest, will be
"directly" involved in a governmental decision. The most relevant subsection of that
regulation appears to be (a)(2), which states:

"(2) The governmental decision involves the zoning or
rezoning, annexation or deannexation, sale, purchase, or lease, or
inclusion in or exclusion from any city, county, district or other local
governmental subdivision, of the real property in which the official has
an interest or a similar decision affecting the real property. For
purposes of this subdivision, the terms "zoning" and "rezoning" shall
refer to the act of establishing or changing the zoning or land use
designation on the real property in which the official has an interest."

In a governmental decision on a City-wide zoning amendment that would restrict
how visible recreational vehicles may be from the street, your home initially appears to
be directly involved. But subdivision (b) of regulation 18704.2 contains three categories
of exceptions to subdivision (a). Most pertinent is subsection (b)(1) of regulation
18704.2, which states:

File No. I-06-152
Page No. 7

"(b) Notwithstanding subdivision (a) above, real property in which a public official has an interest is not directly involved in a governmental decision, but is instead indirectly involved if:
"(1) The decision solely concerns the amendment of an existing zoning ordinance or other land use regulation (such as changes in the uses permitted, or development standards applicable, within a particular zoning category) which is applicable to all other properties designated in that category, which shall be analyzed under 2 Cal. Code Regs. section 18705.2(b)."

Since, in your question, you describe the decision at issue to be a "zoning amendment," the exception in regulation 18704.2(b)(1) applies and therefore, your home is "indirectly" involved with the decision.

B. Whether A Source Of Income Is Directly Or Indirectly Involved.

In analyzing whether a source of income is directly or indirectly involved in a decision before an official's agency, we look to regulation 18704.1(a), which states in pertinent part:

"(a) A person, including business entities, sources of income, and sources of gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an official's agency when that person, either directly or by an agent:

"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;

"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the decision before the official or the official's agency. A person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person."

Stated more specifically, a source of income is directly involved in a decision before an official's agency when that source of income, directly or by an agent, is a named party in, or is the subject of, a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the source of income. (Reg. 18704.1(a).)

File No. 1-06-152
Page No. 8

You have not indicated that the real estate brokerage you work for, in any way,
meets either of the tests set forth in regulation 18704.1(a). Also, you indicate that Mr.
Davis, a Cerritos resident who lives in a different area of the City, is the person who has
requested that the City consider the recreational vehicle zoning amendment. However,
we have no facts indicating that Mr. Davis (or any other person who may meet the above
tests) constitutes a source of income to you through your work as a real estate agent.

C. Whether Personal Finances Are Directly Or Indirectly Involved.

Since a "governmental decision which has any financial effect" on a public
official (or his or her immediate family) is deemed to be directly involved, your
economic interest probably comes within the ambit of the "personal financial effects"
rule and, therefore, this economic interest is deemed to be directly involved. (Reg.
18704.5 [italics added].)

Step 5: Is The Financial Effect Of The Governmental Decision On Your
Economic Interest Material?

A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable impact of a
governmental decision on a public official's economic interests is material. (Regs.
18700(a), 18705.) Different standards apply to determine whether a financial effect will
be material, depending upon the nature of the economic interest and whether that interest
is directly or indirectly involved in the governmental decision under consideration. (Reg.
18705.2, subds.(a) and (b).)

A. Whether The Financial Effect On The Real Property Is Material.

The reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on
indirectly involved real property is presumed not to be material. (Reg. 18705.2(b)(1).)
Because you have an economic interest in your home, and your home is indirectly
involved in the governmental decision you describe, the financial effect of the decision is
presumed not to be material. However, that presumption can be rebutted by the
presentation of certain facts. Regulation 18705.2(b)(1) also states, in pertinent part:

"This presumption may be rebutted by proof that there are specific
circumstances regarding the governmental decision, its financial effect
and the nature of the real property in which the public official has an
economic interest, which make it reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect on the real property in
which the public official has an interest. Examples of specific
circumstances that will be considered include, but are not limited to,
circumstances where the decision affects:

File No. I-06-152
Page No. 9

"(A) The development potential or income producing potential of
the real property in which the official has an economic interest;

"(B) The use of the real property in which the official has an
economic interest;

"(C) The character of the neighborhood including, but not limited
to, substantial effects on: traffic, view, privacy, intensity of use, noise
levels, air emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood."

This is a fact-sensitive inquiry that could result in a finding that the effect of your
participation in a governmental decision, regarding a zoning amendment restricting the
permissible visibility of recreation vehicles in the City, could have either a material or not
have a material effect on the economic value of your home. Note, in particular, that a
decision which substantially affects "[t]he character of the neighborhood including, but
not limited to, substantial effects on: . . . view" could rebut the presumption of non-
materiality. (Reg. 18705.2(b)(1)(C).) This is a determination that you must make.

B. Whether The Financial Effect On The Source Of Income Is Material.

The reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on an
indirectly involved source of income, which is a business entity (as is the real estate
brokerage for which you work as a real estate agent) is determined by applying the
materiality standards applicable to that particular business entity under regulation
18705.1(c). (Reg. 18705.3(b)(1).)

Whether the financial effect of a governmental decision on an indirectly involved
business entity is material or not depends upon what type of business entity is involved.
(See reg. 18705.1(c)(1) – (4), copy enclosed.) There are different tests depending upon
whether the business entity in question is listed in the Fortune 500, on the New York
Stock Exchange, NASDAQ, or American Stock Exchange. (Ibid.) Generally, if a business
entity does not fit any of the preceding lists, it is sometimes referred to as a "small
business entity" and regulation 18705.1(c)(4) applies.

Assuming the real estate brokerage that you work for as an agent (and which
serves as your identified source of income) is determined to be a small business entity
(see reg. 18705.1(c)(4)), the financial effects of a governmental decision will be
considered material if that business is affected in one or more of the following described
ways:

"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the
amount of $20,000 or more; or,

File No. I-06-152
Page No. 10

"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating
existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or

"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease
in the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or
more." (Reg. 18705.1(c)(4).)

Because you have not provided us with: (1) facts regarding what type of business
entity the real estate brokerage you work for is, or (2) facts indicating what the effect of
the specific decision described in your correspondence might have on the real estate
brokerage for which you work, you must analyze – using the proper standards described
above – whether the financial effect of the described governmental decision would have a
material effect on your identified source of income.

C. Whether The Financial Effect On Your Personal Finances Is Material.

Regulation 18705.5(a) sets out the relevant standard as follows:

"The reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental
decision on a public official's personal finances is material if it is at least
$250 in any 12-month period. When determining whether a
governmental decision has a material financial effect on a public
official's economic interest in his or her personal finances, neither a
financial effect on the value of real property owned directly or indirectly
by the official, nor a financial effect on the gross revenues, expenses, or
value of assets and liabilities of a business entity in which the official
has a direct or indirect investment interest shall be considered."

Therefore, if the decision at issue has a reasonably foreseeable financial
effect, and that effect only impacts the value of your home or your business
interests, the effect will not be considered. However, if the decision had a
reasonably foreseeable financial effect on your sources of income as a real estate
agent, then it could be deemed material if the effect were $250 or more in any 12-
month period.

Step 6: Is The Material Financial Effect Reasonably Foreseeable?

If a material financial effect on any of your economic interests is reasonably
foreseeable (i.e., substantially likely to occur), then – without considering the application
of any possible exceptions – you will be deemed to have a conflict of interest under the
Act. (See reg. 18706.) A material financial effect on an economic interest is "reasonably
foreseeable" if it is substantially likely that one or more of the materiality standards
applicable to the particular economic interest under analysis will be met as a result of the

File No. 1-06-152
Page No. 11

governmental decision. (Reg. 18706(a).) A financial effect need not be certain to be
considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere possibility. (In re
Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198; see Reg. 18706(b) [listing five factors to consider in
determining whether the material financial effect of a governmental decision will be
reasonably foreseeable].)

Subsections (b) & (c), of regulation 18706, which set out a list of non-exclusive
factors to consider in determining whether a financial effect is reasonably foreseeable,
serve as a general guideline to use in determining whether this standard is met. Those
subsections state:

"(b) In determining whether a governmental decision will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on an economic interest as defined in subdivision (a) above, the following factors should be
considered. These factors are not intended to be an exclusive list of the
relevant factors that may be considered in determining whether a financial
effect is reasonably foreseeable, but are included as general guidelines:

"(1) The extent to which the official or the official's source of
income has engaged, is engaged, or plans on engaging in business
activity in the jurisdiction;

"(2) The market share held by the official or the official's source of
income in the jurisdiction;

"(3) The extent to which the official or the official's source of
income has competition for business in the jurisdiction;

"(4) The scope of the governmental decision in question; and

"(5) The extent to which the occurrence of the material financial
effect is contingent upon intervening events, not including future
governmental decisions by the official's agency, or any other agency
appointed by or subject to the budgetary control of the official's agency.

"(c) Possession of a real estate sales or brokerage license, or any
other professional license, without regard to the official's business
activity or likely business activity, does not in itself make a material
financial effect on the official's economic interest reasonably
foreseeable."

The factors presented above, and any other potentially relevant factors, must be
examined against each economic interest identified. Because such an analysis involves a
fact-sensitive inquiry, and we do not have enough facts regarding the relevant inquiry
(e.g., what type of business entity the real estate brokerage you work for is, under

File No. I-06-152
Page No. 12

regulation 18705.1(c)), we cannot analyze whether a material financial effect on any of
your identified economic interests is reasonably foreseeable.

Steps 7 & 8: The "Public Generally" And "Legally Required" Exceptions.

If an official has a conflict-of-interest regarding a proposed governmental
decision, he or she may still participate in the described governmental decision making
process if either the "public generally" or "legally required participation" exceptions
apply. (See regs. 18707, 18708.) Though you have not provided us with facts relevant to
these exceptions, we briefly mention them in case you were not aware that they exist.

If a public official has more than one, identifiable economic interest giving rise to
a conflict of interest, the exceptions must apply to each economic interest to render the
conflicts non-disqualifying. If neither exception applies, the conflicts of interest are
deemed disqualifying conflicts and the public official will not be able to make, participate
in making or influencing the governmental decisions described under the Act. Because
you have not provided us with the facts necessary to analyze your situation under these
exceptions, the following information is provided as a general guide.

The "Public Generally" Exception

A public official will not be disqualified from participating in a governmental
decision if the proposed decision affects the public official's economic interests in a
manner which is indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect the
"public generally." (Section 87103; reg. 18707(a).) Under the two prong test, to meet
the requirements of this exception, the decision must financially affect a "significant
segment" of the public generally in "substantially the same manner" that it would affect
you. (Reg. 18707.1.) Different tests apply, depending upon the character of the
economic interest being examined.⁸

For example, with regard to decisions that "affect the personal expenses, income,
assets, or liabilities of a public official or a member of his or her immediate family, or
that affect an individual who is a source of income," the decision will affect a "significant
segment" of the public generally if: (1) ten percent or more of the population in the
jurisdiction of the official's agency or the district the official represents; or (2) 5,000
individuals who are residents of the jurisdiction. (Reg. 18707.1(b)(1)(A))⁹

⁸ Unless every financial interest giving rise to a conflict of interest under the first six steps meets
the public generally (or another) exception, then you are disqualified from participating in the decision.

⁹ You indicate that there are approximately 200 recreational vehicles stored on residential lots in
Cerritos, and that there are approximately 15,000 homes in Cerritos. If you determine that those 200
recreational vehicles affect at least 10%, or 5,000 residents, in the City, than the significant segment portion
of the public generally exception test is met.

File No. I-06-152
Page No. 13

With regard to real property interests, the "significant segment" is defined as 10% of property owners or homeowners in the jurisdiction, or the district the official represents, or 5,000 property owners or homeowners in the jurisdiction. (Reg. 18707.1(b)(1)(B).)¹⁰ If either of these tests applies, then it must be determined whether the significant segment is affected in "substantially the same manner" as the public official's economic interest. (Reg. 18707.1(b)(2).)

Again, because we do not have sufficient facts to apply this exception, we are unable to provide further analysis.

The "Legally Required Participation" Exception

You have not provided any facts suggesting that this exception would apply.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Andreas C. Rockas
Counsel, Legal Division

ACR:rd
I:\AdviceLtrs\06-152

¹⁰ If you determine that those 200 recreational vehicles affect over 1,500 property owners or homeowners in the City, than the significant segment part of the public generally exception test is met.

or the financial interests of persons who have supported them.', (Section g1001(b).)
Section 87100 prohibits any public official frorn-makin g, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influenc" gorr"*-ental decisions in which
the official has a financial interest, unless an exception applies.
In a telephone conversation you indicated that you own a small urban plaruring company, but,
because you do not make (and have not made) any money from that business *hil" yo., Ir. u itr.*irrg
commissioner, that you will not have earned $500 or moie from this dormant business n the l2months
preceding the decision is made.
The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether
an official has a disqualif,ring conflict of interest. (Reg. 1g700@).) The general *1",
however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public ofnciat *uf", u
governmental decision which has a reasonably foreseeable mateialfinancial effect on
one or more of his or her financial interests. (section s7103.)
Steps 1 & 2: As A Planning Commissioner, Are You A public Official
Making, Participating rn Making, or rnfluencing A Governmental Decision?
As a planning commissioner, you are a member or officer of a local government
agency and are a public official under the Act. (Section g204g; see reg. nl6t6y
[defining "public official'].)a As a planning commissioner you will be called upon to
make, patticipate in making, and/or influence governmental decisions regarding the
zoning amendment you have described. (See regs. 18702 - 18702.4.) Tf,erefore, you
w1!1be making, pat'ticipating in making, or otherwise using or attempting to use your
official position to influence a govemmental decision. (See regs. 1 g7o2.l - rcl6i.lSt
Step 3: Do You Ifave A Potentially Disqualifying Economic Interest?
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of
section 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on any of the
following types of interests:
a Ifa public ofEcial's office is listed in section 87200 ("87200 filers,, include city council
members and members ofplanning commissions) and he or she has a conflict of interest in a decision
noticed at a public meeting, then he or she must:.(l) immediately prior to the discussion of the item,
verbally identi$r eachtype of economic interest involved in the iecision as well as details of the economic
interest, as discussed inregulation 18702.5(bXl)(B), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or
herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed
sessions, consent calendars, absences and speaking as a member ofthe public regarding personal interests,
special rules found in regulation 18702.5, subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (Section szr6i.y Ifyou want to
speak as a mernber of thepublic, you may speak_only on behalf of yi), p"rsonrl interests. (deg.
18702.4(b).) You may not speak in a manner which:, through roTi, o, actions, would lead a listener
reasonably to believe that you are speabing on behalf of aiother person or entity, e.g., the planning
Commission. (Ibid.; See Hensley Advice Letter, No- a-o+- t 08.) Since you are a member of the planning
Commission, these requirements are applicable to you if you deiermine that you have a conflict-of-interest
in a governmental decision.
s Actions by a public official that could 99nslitute "attempting to use his or her official position to
influence" a govemmental decision before that o-fficial's agency incluie actions as innocuous as merely
contacting or appearing before "any member, officer, "*ptoy"" or consultant of the agency,, if the purpose
of the contact is to influence the decision. (See reg. tgl}2.i.)
o An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or
indirect investment6 of $2,000 or more (section S7lO3(a);reg. 18703.1(a)), or in
which he or she is a director, officer, partner,trustee, 
"mptoy"e, or holds'any
position of management (section 87103(d); reg. 1g703.1(b)
o An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more (section 87103(b); reg. 1 8703.2; see section 82033
[defining "Interest in Real property',]);
o An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income, which
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to thi decision (section
871 03(c); reg. 1 87 03.3);
o An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to
5360 or more within 72 morrths prior to the decision (section SZlO:G);rei.
18703.4);
o An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her
immediate family -- this is the 'personal financial effects'rule. (Section g7103;
reg. 18703.5.)
Based on the facts you have provided, we can identiff three economic interests to
analyze.
A. Real Properfy.
Your Home. Because you own your home, and your home is worth at least
$2,000, your home constitutes an economic interest.,.rd", the Act. (Sections g2033 and
871 03(b); reg. 187 03.2.)
B. Business Entities And Other Persons Who Are Source Of Income.
Your Income Received As A Real Estate Agent. A public official has a financial
interest in a decision within the meaning of section 87103lf it ir reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have amateial financial effect on any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregatesto $500 or more within l2 months prior to the
decision' (Section 87103(c);reg. 18703.3.) Because yourjob as arealLstate agent, with
clients in the City, will provide you with income (including promised income) of $500 or
more withinthe 12 months prior to the governmental decision at issue, yourlob
constitutes an economic interest under the Act. (Section g7103(c); reg.-tszo3.:.)
6 Fot pu.poses of section 87lo3,"indirect investment or interest means any investment or interest
owned by the spouse or dependent child of a public-official, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by
a business entity or trust in which the official, the official's aglnts, ,!or.rr", and dependent children own
directly, indirectly, or beneficially a l0-percent interest or grJater." (Section 8710:, lastparagraph.)
Regulation 18703.3(c) contains disclosure and disqualification requirements for
any public official who receives commission income for services rendered as a real estate
agent. "Commission income" means gross payments received by a public official as a
result ofservices rendered as a broker, agent, or other salesperson for a specific sale or
similar transaction. (Reg. 15703.3(c)(1).) Commission income is received when it is
paid or credited. (Ibid.)
Note that, under this regulation, anumber of parties in a real estate transaction are
considered sources of income for a real estate agent. (See reg. 18703.3(c)(2)(C).) That
regulation provides that the following persons and entities are all sources'ofincome, in a
specific sale or similar transaction, under the Act:
"(i) The broker and brokerage business entity under whose auspices the
agent works;
"(ii) The person the agent represents in the transaction; and
"(iii) Any person who receives a finder's or other referral fee for referring
a parly to the transaction to the broker, or who makes a referral pursuant to a
contract with the broker." (Ibid.)
You have an economic interest in the brokerage that you work under, as a source
of income, assuming you have received income from the brokerage which aggregates to
$500 or more within 12 months prior to the time the relevant decision wi1 belade.
Other persons referenced in regulation 18703.3(cX2XC) may also be sources of income
to you, of course, if you receive or ar: promised $500 or more by any of them for
services described in this regulation.T
As previously stated, we do not have information regarding what individual
persons you will have received at least $500 from through your representation of them in
real estate transactions in the 12 months prior to the decision. tn adaition, we do not have
information regarding what, if any,persons in connection with your work as a real estate
agent, will have received a finder's fee (or other referral fee) for referring aparty to the
transaction to the broker, or who will have made a referral pursuant to a contract with the
broker that meets or exceeds the $500 / L2-monthlimit. Therefore, with regard to your
real estate agent commission income, we will continue to examine only the-real estate
brokerage you work for as an identifiable economic interest under the Act.
7 We assume that Mr. Davis is not a source of income to you.
C. Personal Finances.
You have an economic interest in yourpersonal finances and those of your
immediate family. (Section 87103;reg.18703.5; see section 82029 [defining..immediate
family" as one's spouse and dependent children].) Because you provide ur ,rith no facts
regarding the personal finances of your immediate family, we simply identifii your
personal finances as a category of economic interest relevant onlylo you.
Step 4: Are The Economic Interests Directly Or Indirectly Involved In The
Governmental Decision?
Once an official identifies an economic interest, he or she must determine whether
the economic interest is directly or indirectly involved in the specific decision in
question. (Regs. 18700(bX4),18704(a).) Having established the degree of involvement,
the official can then identi$z the materiality standard appropriate to the circumstances.
(Reg. 18700(bX5).) There are separate tests applied to real property, sources of income,
and personal finances, to determine whether an economic interest is directly or indirectly
involved in a specific govemmental decision. We first address the test appiicable to reai
property.
A. whether Real Property rs Direcfly or rndirectly rnvolved.
Regulation L870a.2@) states six general categories of governmental decisions, or
tlpes of real property, in which a public official has an economic interest, will be
"directly" involved in a governmental decision. The most relevant subsection of that
regulation appears to be (a)(2), which states:
"(2) The govemmental decision involves the zoning or
rezoning, annexation or deannexation, sale, purchase, or lease, or
inclusion in or exclusion from any city, county, district or other local
governmental subdivision, of the real property in which the official has
an interest or a similar decision affecting the rear property. For
purposes of this subdivision, the terms *zoning,, and.,,tezoning,, shall
refer to the act of establishing or changing the zoningor land use
designation on the real property in which the official has an interest.,'
In a governmental decision on a City-wide zoningamendment that would restrict
how visible recreational vehicles may be from the street, your home inttially appears to
be directly involved. But subdivision (b) of regulation 18704.2 contains three^categories
of exceptions to subdivision (a). Most pertinent is subsection (b)(l) of regulation
),87 04.2, which states:
"(b) Notwithstanding subdivision (a) above, real property in
which a public official has an interest is not directly involved in a
govefirmental decision, but is instead indirectly involved if
"(1) The decision solely concerns the amendment of an existing
zoning ordinance or other land use regulation (such as changes in the
uses permitted, or development standards applicable, within aparticular
zoning category) which is applicable to all other properties designated in
that category, which shall be analyzedunder 2 cal code Regs. section
t8705.2(b)."
Since, in your question, you describe the decision at issue to be a "zoning
amendment," the exception in regulation 18704.2(b)(1) applies and therefore, your home
is "indirectly''involved with the decision.
B. Whether A Source Of Income Is Directly Or Indirecfly fnvolved.
In analyzing whether a source of income is directly or indirectly involved in a
decision before an official's agency, we look to regulation 18704.1(a), which states in
pertinent part:
"(a) A person, including business entities, sources of income, and
sources of gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an official's
agency when that person, either directly or by an agent:
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by
filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official,s agency. A
person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license,
permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person.,,
Stated more specifically, a source of income is directly involved in a decision
before an official's agency when that source of income, directly or by an agent, is a
named patty in, or is the subject of, a proceeding if the decision invoives tlie issuance,
renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with, the source of income. (Reg. 1870a.1@).)
You have not indicated that the real estate brokerage you work for, in any way,
meets either of the tests set out in regulation 18704.1(a). Also, you indicate that Mr.
Davis, a Cerritos resident who lives in a different area of the City, is the person who has
requested that the City consider the recreational vehicle zoningamendment. However,
we have no facts indicating that Mr. Davis (or any other person who may meet the above
tests) constitutes a source of income to you through your work as areal estate agent.
C. Whether Personal Finances Are Directly Or Indirectly fnvolved.
Since a "governmental decision which has any financial effect" on a public
official (or his or her immediate family) is deemed to be directly involved, your
economic interest probably comes within the ambit of the "personal financial effects"
rule and, therefore, this economic interest is deemed tobe directly involved. (Reg.
187 04.5 [italics added].)
Step 5: Is The Financial Effect Of The Governmental Decision On Your
Economic Interest Material ?
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable impact of a
govefitmental decision on a public official's economic interests is material. (Regs.
18700(a), 18705.) Different standards apply to determine whether a financial effect will
be material, depending upon the nature of the economic interest and whether that interest
is directly or indirectly involved in the govemmental decision under consideration. (Reg.
18705.2, subds.(a) and (b).)
A. whether The Financial Effect on The Real Properfy rs Material.
The reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a govemmental decision on
indirectly involved real property is presumed not to be material. (Reg. 18705.2(bxl).)
Because you have an economic interest in your home, and your home is indirectly
involved in the govemmental decision you describe, the financial effect of the decision is
presumed not to be material. However, that presumption can be rebutted by the
presentation of certain facts. Regulation 18705.2(b)(1) also states, in pertinentpart:
"This presumption may be rebutted by proof that there are specific
circumstances regarding the governmental decision, its financial effect,
and the nature of the real property in which the public official has an
economic interest, which make it reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have amateial financial effect on the real property in
which the public official has an interest. Examples of specific
circumstances that will be considered include, but are not limited to,
circumstances where the decision affects:
"(A) The development potential or incorne producing potential of
the real property in which the official has an economic inlerest;
"(B) The use of the rear property in which the official has an
economic interest;
"(C) The character of the neighborhood including, but not limited
to, substantial effects on: traffic, view, pivacy,intensity of use, noise
levels, air emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood.,,
This is a fact-sensitive inquiry that could result in a finding that the effect of your
participation in a govefirmental decision, regarding a zoningu.rr"rid.o".rt restricting tle
permissible visibility of recreation vehicles in the City, could have either amaterialor not
have amateial effect on the economic value of your Lo-e. Note, in particular , that a
decision which substantially affects "[t]he character of the neighborhood including, but
not limited to, substantial effects on: . . . view" could rebut the presumption of non-
materiality. (Reg. 18705.2(b)(1XC).) This is a determination tirat you'must make.
B. Whether The Financial Effect On The Source Of Income Is Material.
The reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on an
indirectly involved source of income, which is a business entity (as is the real estate
brokerage for which you work as a real estate agent) is determined by applying the
mateiality standards applicable to that particular business entity undlr iegulation
1 8705. 1 (c). (Ree. 1 870s.3(bx1).)
Whether the financial effect of a governmental decision on an indirectly involved
business entity is material or not depends upon what type of business entity is involved.
(See reg. 18705.1(c)(1) - (4), copy enclosed.) There are different tests depending upon
whether the business entity in question is listed in the Forfune 500, on the New york
Stock Exchange, NASDAQ, American Stock Exchange. (rbid.) Generally, if a business
entity does not frt any of the preceding lists, it is sometimes referred to as a..small
business entity" and regulation I8705.I(c)(a) applies.
Assuming the real estate brokerage that you work for as an agent (and which
serves as your identified source of income) is determined to be a.-ull business entity
(see reg. 18705.1(c)(4)), the financial effects of a governmental decision will be
considered mateial if that business is affected in one or more of the following described
ways:
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the
amount of $20,000 or more; or,
"(B) The goveflrmental decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating
existing expenses for a fiscal year inthe amount of $5,000 or more; or
"(C) The govemmental decision will result in an increase or decrease
in the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or
more." (Reg. 1 8705. 1 (c)(a).)
Because you have not provided us with: (1) facts regarding what type of business
entity the real estate brokerage you work for is, or (2) facts indicaiing wtraittre effect of
the specific decision described in your correspondence might have on the real estate
brokerage for which you work, you must analyze- using th. p.op", standards described
above - whether the financial effect of the described gove-mental decision would have a
material effect on your identified source of income.
C. Whether The Financial Effect On Your Personal Finances Is Material.
Regulation 18705.5(a) sets out the relevant standard as follows:
"The reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a govemmental
decision on a public official's personal finances is material if it is at least
$250 in any L2-month period. When determining whether a
goverrmental decision has a material financial effect on a public
official's economic interest in his or her personal finances, neither a
financial effect on the value of real property owned directly or indirectly
by the official, nor a financial effect on the gross revenues, expenses, or
value of assets and liabilities of a business entity in which the official
has a direct or indirect investment interest shall be considered.,,
Therefore, if the decision at issue has a reasonably foreseeable financial
effect, and that effect only impacts the value of your home or your business
interests, the effect will not be considered. However, if the decision had a
reasonably foreseeable financial effect on your sources of income as a real estate
agent, then it could be deemed material if the effect were $250 or more in any 12-
rnonth period.
Step 6: rs The Material Financiar Effect Reasonably Foreseeable?
If a material financial effect on any of your economic interests is reasonably
foreseeable (i.e., substantially likely to occur), then - without considering the application
of any possible exceptions - you will be deemed to have a conflict of intJrest under the
Act. (See reg. 18706.) A material financial effect on an economic interest is ..reasonably
foreseeable" if it is substantially likely that one or more of the materiality standards
applicable to the particulat economic interest under analysis will be met as a result of the
govefirmental decision. (Reg. 18706(a).) A financial effect need not be certain to be
considered reasonably foreseeeable, but it must be more than amere possibility. (In re
Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198; see Reg. 18706(b) [listing five faciors to consider in
determining whether the material financial effect of a governmental decision will be
reasonably foreseeablel. )
Subsections (b) & (c), of regulation l8706,which set out a list of non-exclusive
factors to consider in determining whether a financial effect is reasonably foreseeable,
serve as a general guideline to use in determining whether this standard is met. Those
subsections state:
"(b) In determining whether a govefilmental decision will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on an economic interest
as defined in subdivision (a) above, the following factors should be
considered. These factors are not intended to be an exclusive list of the
relevant facts that may be considered in determining whether a financial
effect is reasonably foreseeable, but are included as general guidelines:
"(1) The extent to which the official or the official's source of
income has engaged, is engaged, or plans on engaging in business
activity in the jurisdiction;
"(2) The market share held by the official or the official's source of
income in the jurisdiction;
"(3) The extent to which the official or the official,s source of
income has competition for business in the jurisdiction;
"(4) The scope of the governmental decision in question; and
"(5) The extent to which the occurrence of the material financial
effect is contingent upon intervening events, not including future
govemmental decisions by the official's agency, or any other agency
appointed by or subject to the budgetary control of theofficial,i agency.
"(c) Possession ofa real estate sales or brokerage license, or a11y
other professional license, without regard to the official's business 
'
activity or likely business activity, does not in itself make a material
financial effect on the official's economic interest reasonably
foreseeable."
The factors presented above, and. any other potentially relevant factors, must be
examined against each economic interest identified. Because such an analysisinvolves a
fact-sensitive inquiry, and we do not have enough facts regarding the relevant inquiry
(e.g., what type of business entity the real estate brokerage you work for is, under
regulation 18705-1(c)), we cannot analyze whether amatenal financial effect on any of
your identified economic interests is reasonably foreseeable.
steps 7 & 8: The "public Generally,, And orLegallyRequired, Exceptions.
If an official has a conflict-of-interest regarding a proposed governmental
decision, he or she may still participate in the described gou"--"rriir a""irio" making
process if either the-*public generally" or "legally requirid participation,, exceptions
apply' (See regs. 18707,18708.) Though you have not provided us with facts relevant to
these exceptions, we briefly mention them in case you were not awarethat they exist.
If a public official has more than one, identifiable economic interest giving rise to
a conflict of interest, the exceptions must apply to each economic interest to render the
conflicts non-disqualifyrng. If neither rr""ption applies, the conflicts of interest are
deemed disqualifying conflicts and the public official will not be able to make, participate
in making or influencing the goveflrmental decisions described under the Act. Because
you have not provided us with the facts necessary to analyzeyour sifuation under these
exceptions, the following information is provided ur u g"r"rui guide.
The "Public Generally" Exceptton
A public official will not be disqualified from participatin gin agovernmental
decision if the proposed decision affects the public official's economic ilterests in a
manner which is indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect the
"public generally." (section 87r03 reg. I s707(a).) Under the two prong test, to meet
the requirements of this exception, the decision must financially afficta;significant
segment" of the public generally in "substantially the same mann er,, thatit would affect
you. (Reg. 18707 .1.) Different tests apply, depending upon the character of the
economic interest being examined.s
For example, with regard to decisions that "affect the personal expenses, income,
assets, or liabilities of a public official or a member of his o, h., immediate family, or
that affect an individual who is a source of income," the decision will affect a..significant
segment" of the pub^llc generally if: (1) ten percent or more of the population in the
jurisdiction of the official's agency or the district the official represents; or (2) 5,000
individuals who are residents of the jurisdiction. (Reg. tg70z.i(uxrXa).f '-'
8 unless every financial interest giving rise to a conflict of interest under the first six steps meets
the public generally (or another) exception, then you are disqualified from participating in the decision.
e You indicate that there are approxim ately 2l}recreational vehicles stored on residential lots in
cerritos, and that there are approximately 15,000 homes in Cerritos. If you determine that those 200
recreational vehicles affect atleast 10o/o, or 5,000 residents, in the City, than the signlficant segment portion
of the public generally exception test is met.
With regard to real property interests, the "significant segment" is define d. as 70%o
of property owners or homeowners in the jurisdiction, or the disirict the official
represents, or 5,000 property owners or homeowners in the jurisdiction. (Reg.
18707 '1(bX1XB).) " If either of these tests applies, then it must be determin-ed whether
th-e-significant segment is affected in "substantially the same manner,, as the public
official's economic interest. (Reg. 1 8707.1(b)(2).)
Again, because we do not have sufficient facts to apply this exception, we are
unable to provide further analysis.
The "Legally Required Participation " Exception
You have not provided any facts suggesting that this exception would apply.

QUESTION: You are a planning commissioner considering a city-wide zoning amendment that
would restrict how visible recreational vehicles may be from the street. -Cu, yo, make,
participate in making, or attempt to use your official position to influence thodescribed
decision?

CONCLUSION: Based upon the facts you have provided, it appears that you mayhave a
disqualitring conflict of interest, depending upon, eg., the nature of the real estate
brokerage you work for and the effect such an amendment would have on the value of

embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 September 20, 2006 Diana Needham Vice-Chair Cerritos Planning Commission 12927 Lucas Lane Cerritos, California 90703 RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-06-152 Dear Ms. Needham: This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because your request does not specify all the facts necessary for a complete analysis of the economic interests described, we can only provide you with informal assistance.² Please note that the Fair Political Practices Commission does not act as a finder of fact when providing advice; this advice is based solely on the facts that you provide. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) QUESTION You are a planning commissioner considering a city-wide zoning amendment that would restrict how visible recreational vehicles may be from the street. Can you make, participate in making, or attempt to use your official position to influence the described decision? CONCLUSION Based upon the facts you have provided, it appears that you may have a disqualifying conflict of interest, depending upon, e.g., the nature of the real estate brokerage you work for and the effect such an amendment would have on the value of your home and the residents of your city, generally. ___________________________ ¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. ² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; reg. 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.) File No. 1-06-152 Page No. 2 FACTS You are a planning commissioner for the City of Cerritos (the "City") and Vice- Chair of the Cerritos Planning Commission (the "Planning Commission"). Mr. Davis, a Cerritos resident, who lives in a different area of the City than you, has requested that the city council consider adopting a city-wide zoning amendment that would restrict the parking of recreational vehicles on private property so as to make them less visible (or not visible at all) when viewed from the street. Mr. Davis is upset about a large recreational vehicle parked next to his house. The city council has directed the matter to the Planning Commission to explore crafting the zoning amendment proposed by Mr. Davis. You own a home, with an interest of at least $2,000. Your home is across the street from a house where a recreational vehicle is stored and faces your home. You will earn at least $500 from the brokerage you do work for as a real estate agent, doing business in and around the City, in the 12 months prior to the governmental decision at issue.³ You indicated on the telephone that you do not have an equity interest in the brokerage for which you work. We do not know the identity of any person from which you will have received at least $500
